Defendant The Realreal, Inc.'S Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Proceedings
Off Calendar Feb-21-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Wendy M. Lazerson, Esq., Sidley Austin LLP, Telephone 650-5657065, appearing for Defendant The Real;
No other appearance.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant The RealReal Inc.'s motion to compel arbitration and staying this action is granted. Defendant's motion for an order awarding attorneys' fees and costs as sanctions is denied.

Plaintiff Toni Lessler expressly consented to the Mutual Arbitration Agreement ("MAA" or "the agreement") by signing it. Defendant's signature on the MAA was not a requirement for Defendant to enforce the agreement to compel arbitration. (See Serafin v. Balco Properties Ltd., LLC (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 165, 176 ["[T]he writing memorializing an arbitration agreement need not be signed by both parties in order to be upheld as a binding arbitration agreement. . . . [I]t is not the presence or absence of a signature [on an agreement] which is dispositive; it is the presence or absence of evidence of an agreement to arbitrate which matters."]; Fagelbaum & Heller LLP v. Smylie (2009) 174 Cal.App.4th 1351, 1367 [plaintiff "did not meet his burden to prove 'that the signatures of all parties were contemplated as being a condition precedent to the validity of the contract.' [Citation.] His signature individually bound him to the agreement and to the arbitration clause within it."]; Vargas v. Delivery Outsourcing LLC (N.D. Cal. Mar. 14, 2016) 2016 WL 946112 at *5 [arbitration agreement was binding on employee although it was not signed by defendant employer].) The existence of an empty employer signature block and the language stating that modifications to the MAA must be evidenced by a mutually signed writing do not render the MAA unenforceable. The MAA contains explicit language predicating enforceability on Plaintiff's signature, but no reciprocal language refers to a need for Defendant's signature. Plaintiff's reliance on Huckaba v. Ref-Chem (5th Cir. 2018) 892 F.3d 686 is misplaced as that case applied Texas law, and in any event is factually distinguishable. The court in that case recognized that "whether a signature is required to bind the parties is a question of the parties' intent," and that signatures are not required "[a]s long as the parties give their consent to the terms of the contract, and there is no evidence of an intent to require both signatures as a condition precedent to it becoming effective as a contract." (Id. at 689.) The arbitration agreement in that case involved explicit language conditioning arbitration on the signatures of both parties; such language is not present here. (See id. ["The arbitration agreement at issue here contains: (1) a statement that '[b]y signing this agreement the parties are giving up any right they may have to sue each other. . . .'"] [emphasis added].)

No sanctions are awarded under Code of Civil Procedure section 128.5 because Plaintiff did not act in bad faith. Specifically, Defendant has not shown that Plaintiff opposed this motion solely to cause delay and the arguments made, while contrary to the weight of California and other precedent, were not "totally and completely without merit." (Code Civ. Proc. 128.5(b)(2).)

Order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: W. Trupek; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano CSR# 8600 email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/EPS)
